entries.title: About the Course
entries.content: Students study various natural features of Canada's provinces and territories and create a detailed, illustrated map over the course of the year. They also read a travelogue from the year's historical period.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: The Canadian mapping exercises are the same each year, but the reading component changes for each of the historical time periods.

rows.label: Geography: Grade 8 (Canada)
rows.books: Canadian Student Atlas||Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada and Greenland: Geography Disguised as Fun||Vanished Beyond the Map

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada and Greenland: Geography Disguised as Fun

blocks.text: The steps below are generally true for most geography lessons. Sometimes several lessons might build together or focus on a particular area (like Map Work).
Through questions, observations, an activity, or recalling the last lesson, introduce the topic for the day.
Answer questions on the map or on making your own maps.
Read the day's passage out loud or have the student read independently.
Have students retell the passage either orally or in writing. They may want to use maps or pictures to tell back.
The lesson may contain more map work as students make their own maps or add to ongoing maps.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

PREP: Print 10 blank maps of Canada. Place one in your student's geography notebook (referred to as "My Map of Canada"). Information will be added over the year. Additional maps are used periodically to see what the student remembers. 
∞ Map Links: Blank Maps of Canada (colour & b/w)

➜ INTRO
This year, we will look at every province and territory of Canada, beginning in the east, then traveling west, and finally north. You will make map entries in your geography notebook about each region, while also creating a large, detailed, and illustrated map. Term 1 is mainly map work, but we will also read excerpts from the journals of three explorers who lived during our history rotation (1800-1900).

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Before we look more closely inside Canada, we're going to start with what is outside. What types of borders does Canada have? [A: land borders and ocean borders]
2\. What country borders Canada to the south? [A: United States of America] What ocean borders Canada to the east? [A: Atlantic Ocean] What ocean borders Canada to the west? [A: Pacific Ocean] What ocean borders Canada to the north? [A: Arctic Ocean] 
3\. Canada also has other borders in the Arctic. What land borders Canada to the northwest? [A: a land border with Alaska, U.S.A.] What land borders Canada to the northeast? [A: a maritime border with Greenland (Denmark)] What body of water is north of Yukon and Alaska? [A: Beaufort Sea] What body of water is south of Yukon and Alaska? [A: Gulf of Alaska] What body of water is between Baffin Island and Greenland? [A: Baffin Bay] 

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
1\. Label the United States of America, the Atlantic Ocean, the Pacific Ocean, and the Arctic Ocean on your map.
2\. Label Alaska, Greenland (Denmark), Beaufort Sea, Gulf of Alaska, and Baffin Bay on your map.
3\. Tape this map into your notebook. Leave the opposite page blank for any notes about Canada you decide to include during the year. Your geography notebook will be used regularly throughout the course. You may be asked to make an entry or to tape your printed maps for Map Work. Ensure that each entry is labeled with the date or lesson number.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.14-31
Maps can convey information in so many different ways beyond borders and place names. Spend some time reading through the types of maps found in this section. 

➜ NOTEBOOK
In your geography notebook, write down some facts you've learned from these maps about your local area and/or home province/territory, such as climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Natural Wonders Map Introduction
⍞ Materials: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland

PREP: Either photocopy the map templates at the end of the book or download them to print. These could be attached to cardboard or poster board to make the map more durable. In the lesson plans, this will be called "My Natural Wonders Map."
∞ Map Link: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland Download

➜ INTRO
With the book Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, you will create your own map of Canada that includes the wonders of the natural world found here, as well as narrative features about geographical elements that are unique to Canada.

➜ READ
1\. p.4 "Instructions." Get an overview of the types of drawings you will need to do to create the map.
2\. p.53 Look at an example of the finished product to see what your map will eventually look like. 

➜ MAP WORK
p.6-7 Follow the instructions to add borders and labels to your map template. Remember the lines and words in red for each lesson are what should be added to your map.

links.text: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland Download

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Canada's Regions
⍞ Materials: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, Canadian Student Atlas, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ INTRO
Today's lesson will look at the different regions of Canada, considering elements such as climate and vegetation.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders p.8-14
1\. Add the eight regional labels and the four directional points listed on these pages to your map. If you decide to shade in the regions, remember you will be adding geographical features on top, so shade lightly or draw an outline in such a way that more information can be added to those spaces later.
2\. Add some notes in your geography notebook about each region. The "Climate Regions" map in the Canadian Student Atlas (p.14) will give additional information about the climate in these regions.

⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.16-17
Make a list in your geography notebook of the different types of vegetation and where they're found in Canada's regions. There will be time next lesson to continue this if you do not have enough time in today's lesson.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Canada
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ NOTEBOOK
Take some time at the beginning of today's lesson to catch up on any notebook entries from the first few lessons.

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
1\. Before learning about each province and territory separately, challenge yourself today to see how much you already know about Canada's geography. Take out one of your blank maps of Canada. Label as many provinces and territories as you remember. Next, label as many capital cities as you can. If you don't know their precise location, just write the name inside the province/territory. Label as many bodies of water as you know, and anything else you can think of. 
2\. Compare your work to the Canadian Student Atlas (p.12-13).

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
New Brunswick
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ INTRO
We will spend at least two classes on each province. In the first class, you will consult the atlas to answer some questions about each province/territory and make entries in your geography notebook. In the second class, you will work on your Natural Wonders Map. If you can't complete everything required in the first class, you can continue working on it in the second.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: New Brunswick
2\. What are the borders of New Brunswick? [A: Quebec, Nova Scotia, United States of America, Gulf of St. Lawrence]
3\. Turn to p.40. What is the capital? [A: Fredericton]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Carleton - 817m - located in the far northwest part of the province]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of New Brunswick in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of New Brunswick using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a couple of the major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
While the notebook entries for each Canadian province/territory are meant to be two pages, the following example from U.S. Geography gives an idea of what could be included on these pages.
∞ Image Link: Example of State Map

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
New Brunswick, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add New Brunswick, Fredericton, and the Bay of Fundy to My Map of Canada in your notebook.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.15-16. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ VIEW 
Watch the tide come in and out at the Bay of Fundy.
∞ Video Link: World's Highest Tides (Time Lapse)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Newfoundland & Labrador
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Newfoundland and Labrador
2\. What are the borders of Newfoundland and Labrador? [A: Quebec, Atlantic Ocean, Labrador Sea, Gulf of St. Lawrence]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [A: St. John's]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Caubvick - 1652m - located in the far northwest part of Labrador on the border with Quebec]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Newfoundland and Labrador in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Newfoundland and Labrador using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a couple of the major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Newfoundland & Labrador, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Newfoundland & Labrador, St. John's, Labrador Sea, and the Gulf of St. Lawrence to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.17-19. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write the definition of a fjord in your notebook.

➜ VIEW
Learn more about Gros Morne National Park, where the fjords at Western Brook Pond can be found.
∞ Video Link: Gros Morne

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Introduction - Adam Shoalts
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas

➜ INTRO
Adam Shoalts (b.1986) has been called "Canada's Indiana Jones," and he is an explorer, adventurer, writer, scholar, archaeologist, and geographer. He serves as the Westaway Explorer-in-Residence at the Royal Canadian Geographical Society. Next term, you will begin reading Vanished Beyond the Map, Shoalts's account of trying to solve the mystery of what happened to Hubert Darrell, a guide, explorer, outdoorsman, and map-maker at the close of the nineteenth century. The Arctic loomed large in nineteenth-century imaginations: the quest to find a Northwest Passage from the Atlantic to the Pacific Ocean and open a faster trade route to Asia resulted in many failed and doomed expeditions, including the Franklin Expedition that departed England in 1845. Decades later, others would make a dangerous journey to the border between Alaska and the Yukon, hoping to strike it rich from the Klondike Gold Rush.
In 2017, in honour of Canada's 150th birthday, Shoalts set off on a solo journey across the Arctic—from Eagle Plain, Yukon to Baker Lake, Nunavut. In order to learn more about Canada's Arctic, much of which was so harsh and unforgiving to nineteenth-century explorers, and to get to know Shoalts, whose book you will read starting next term, several lessons this term will be spent watching the documentary Alone Across the Arctic, an account of Shoalts's solo journey (which he also wrote about in the book Beyond the Trees).

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.32-33 
Locate Eagle Plain, Yukon, and Baker Lake, Nunavut. Shoalts made his trip mainly by canoe and portage. Spend some time looking at the waterways in the Arctic and make a hypothesis for the route he might follow.

⍞ Article Resource: Mapping Adam Shoalts' Across the Arctic Expedition
Read this short article and compare Shoalts's route to your own guess from looking at the atlas.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Alone Across the Arctic
Watch from 0:00-21:53.

• What are your first impressions of Adam Shoalts? What do you think of the plan for his solo voyage?

links.text: Mapping Adam Shoalts' Across the Arctic Expedition

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Hudson Bay
⍞ Materials: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Take out one of your blank maps of Canada and label as many places as you remember from the provinces and territories studied thus far. Then compare your work to the atlas or your notebook.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.20-23. 

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write some facts about the bodies of water you mapped today into your notebook.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Alone Across the Arctic, cont.
⍞ Materials: none

➜ RECAP
Where did you leave Adam Shoalts at the end of the last lesson?

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Alone Across the Arctic
Watch from 21:54-49:49.

• What are some elements of travel through the Arctic that you think would have been especially challenging for nineteenth-century explorers?

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 12
lessons.body:
Nova Scotia
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Nova Scotia
2\. What are the borders of Nova Scotia? [A: New Brunswick, Gulf of St. Lawrence, Atlantic Ocean, and also the Bay of Fundy, which is not labelled on this map]
3\. Turn to p.41. What is the capital? [A: Halifax]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: White Hill - 532m - located on Cape Breton Island]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Nova Scotia in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Nova Scotia using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a couple of the major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 13
lessons.body:

⍞ Materials: none

➜ RECAP

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Alone Across the Arctic
Watch from 49:50-1:14:24.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Nova Scotia, cont.
⍞ Materials: My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Nova Scotia and Halifax to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Natural Wonders map
There are no activities specific to Nova Scotia in the book, so we will add some.
1\. Label Cape Breton Island (consult the atlas if you need to).
2\. Trace the route of the Cabot Trail, with the following map as a reference.
∞ Image Link: Cabot Trail Map

➜ VIEW
Watch the following video showing why the Cabot Trail is so special.
∞ Video Link: Cape Breton Island - The Cabot Trail

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Prince Edward Island
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Prince Edward Island
2\. What are the borders of Prince Edward Island? [A: Northumberland Strait, the Atlantic Ocean]
3\. Turn to p.41. What is the capital? [A: Charlottetown]
4\. How is the island accessed? [A: ferry, Confederation Bridge between New Brunswick and PEI]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Prince Edward Island in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Prince Edward Island using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a couple of the major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Prince Edward Island, cont.
⍞ Materials: My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Prince Edward Island, Charlottetown, and the Northumberland Strait to My Map of Canada.

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Bird's-eye view of beautiful Prince Edward Island

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Natural Wonders map
There are no activities specific to Prince Edward Island in the book, so we will add some.
1\. Prince Edward Island is famous for its beaches. Using the map below, add Cavendish Beach and Brackley Beach to your map.
∞ Map Link: Prince Edward Island Map
2\. With its beaches comes erosion, which can form beautiful structures like Teacup Rock.
∞ Image Link: Teacup Rock.
Sadly, after Hurricane Fiona in 2022, Teacup Rock was washed away.
∞ Article Link: P.E.I.'s iconic Teacup Rock is gone after post-tropical storm Fiona
Using the map below, add Teacup Rock to your map. Print the image link above or draw your own sketch of it.
∞ Map Link: Map of Teacup Rock

links.text: Bird's-eye view of beautiful Prince Edward Island
P.E.I.'s iconic Teacup Rock is gone after post-tropical storm Fiona

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
Québec
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map, My Map of Canada, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Québec
2\. What are the water borders of Québec? [A: Hudson Bay, Hudson Strait, Labrador Sea, the Gulf of St. Lawrence, St. Lawrence River]
3\. What are the land borders of Québec? [A: Ontario, Newfoundland and Labrador, New Brunswick, and the United States of America]
4\. Turn to p.39. What is the capital? [A: Québec City]
5\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mont d'Iberville - 1652m - located in the far northeast part of the province, on the east side of Ungava Bay]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Québec in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Québec using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a couple of the major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11. 

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
You already mapped part of Québec during the week on Hudson Bay. Today, complete the activities on p.24. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ READ
Learn more about Lake Manicouagan and how geologists believe it was formed.
∞ Article Link: Manicouagan Reservoir

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write about what makes Lake Manicouagan so special.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
History of Arctic Exploration - Catch-up
⍞ Materials: none

➜ INTRO
Historical maps provide a window into how knowledge of the Arctic developed over hundreds of years. The Northwest Passage was finally traversed for the first time by Roald Amundsen in 1903-1906. 
For today's lesson, view the maps at the following link and spend the rest of the time on catch-up for this or another course.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE & DISCUSS
∞ Article Link: 10 Fascinating Historic Maps of the Arctic

• What do you notice about how mapmaking has changed over the past 425 years as you look at these maps? When did mapping of the Arctic become the most accurate?

links.text: 10 Fascinating Historic Maps of the Arctic

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
Ontario
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Locate: Ontario
2\. What are the borders of Ontario? [A: Manitoba, Quebec, United States of America, Hudson Bay]
3\. What bodies of water form a natural border to the south? [A: the Great Lakes]
4\. What are the names of the Great Lakes [A: Lake Superior, Lake Michigan, Lake Huron, Lake Erie, and Lake Ontario]
5\. Turn to p.38. What is the capital? [A: Toronto]
6\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Ishpatina Ridge - 693m - located to the west of Haileybury, a town on the Québec border]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Ontario in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Ontario using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, the Great Lakes, a few other major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
Ontario, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.25-29. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Québec, Québec City, Ontario, Toronto, Lake Superior, Lake Michigan, Lake Huron, Lake Erie, Lake Ontario, Niagara Falls, and the St. Lawrence River to My Map of Canada.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 22
lessons.body:
Clay Model of Your Province/Territory
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook, Model Magic clay (or you can make your own salt dough), cardboard or surface for the model, paint, toothpicks or pins

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Take out one of your blank maps of Canada and label as many places as you remember from the provinces and territories studied thus far. Then compare your work to the atlas or your notebook.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.32-42
You will spend today's lesson making a model of your province or territory. You may want to print out a blank map to use as a guide. You can paint your model, put in pins with flags to mark certain areas, or add other creative features. We have included an example for inspiration. 
∞ Image Link: Clay Model Map of Idaho
∞ Image Link: Blank Outline Maps of Canada and Provinces
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
If your student(s) cannot complete the clay map in one class period, they can continue to work on it during exam week.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Manitoba
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Locate: Manitoba
2\. What are the borders of Manitoba? [A: Ontario, Nunavut, Saskatchewan, United States of America]
3\. Turn to p.37. What is the capital? [A: Winnipeg]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Baldy Mountain - 832m - located in the southwest quadrant, west of Winnipegosis]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Manitoba in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Manitoba using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, Lake Winnipeg, a few other major lakes or rivers, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Introduction - Vanished Beyond the Map
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ INTRO
Vanished Beyond the Map is an account of Adam Shoalts's quest to solve the mystery of what happened to Hubert Darrell, a long-forgotten explorer, trapper, guide, and mapmaker, who disappeared in the Arctic in 1910. Shoalts's narrative shows a historian at work: using archival research, historical documents, and interviews with elderly individuals who may have long-ago connections to Darrell's family, Shoalts pieces together Darrell's life and travels. But it is just as much a geographical story: Darrell immigrated from England to Manitoba, then journeyed to the Arctic following the gold rush. His life intersected with that of other great explorers, namely Roald Amundsen and Vilhjalmur Stefansson, and he travelled alone across vast distances in the Arctic.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1\. Vanished Beyond the Map
p.1-11 "Mist hung about" - "story of what I found."

• What are your initial impressions? Do you have any early guesses about where this story might go?

2\. Dictionary of Canadian Biography - Hubert Darrell
Read the same biographical dictionary entry that initially got Adam Shoalts intrigued in the story of Hubert Darrell. If you prefer to avoid spoilers, you can save reading this until later in the year!
∞ Article Link: Darrell, Hubert - Dictionary of Canadian Biography

links.text: Darrell, Hubert - Dictionary of Canadian Biography

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
Manitoba, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Manitoba, Winnipeg, and Lake Winnipeg to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.30-31. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook.

➜ VIEW
Learn more about Churchill, Manitoba.
∞ Video Link: 7 Wonders of Manitoba: Churchill 

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
If you have time, take out one of your blank maps of Canada and label as many places as you remember from the provinces and territories studied thus far. Then compare your work to the atlas or your notebook.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
Immigration to Manitoba, 1890s
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.12-22 "The early details" - "the farm's debts."

• Many immigrants to Canada, especially those continuing westward, came from Europe to Halifax. Imagine the route Darrell might have taken from his family's home in Kent (outside London) to Birtle, Manitoba.

➜ VIEW
Learn more about the landscape Darrell arrived to find in Manitoba by watching some drone footage of Birtle (just skip around and watch a few minutes, as the entire video is quite long).
∞ Video Link: Birtle Manitoba from Above

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
Saskatchewan
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Saskatchewan
2\. What are the borders of Saskatchewan? [A: Manitoba, Northwest Territories, Alberta, United States of America]
3\. What do you notice about Saskatchewan's borders? [A: They are all straight lines; the borders are artificial, and not created by landforms or bodies of water.]
4\. Turn to p.36. What is the capital? [A: Regina]
5\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Cypress Hills - 1468m - located in the southwest corner of the province]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Saskatchewan in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Saskatchewan using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a few bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Klondike
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, Canadian Student Atlas, My Arctic Map

➜ PREP
Print (in colour if possible) this detailed map of Canada's arctic region. If desired, students can add labels and routes to it while they read Vanished Beyond the Map. If printing is not possible, save the link so the map can be referenced easily. In future lessons, this map will be referred to as "My Arctic Map."
∞ Map Link: Yukon and Northwest Territories

➜ RECAP
What do you recall of Darrell's immigration story from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.23-35 "I was shivering" - "a desire to hunt one."

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.32
1\. Locate the Kluane Range [A: They are the mountains between Kluane Lake and the larger St. Elias Mountain Range, the southwest corner of Yukon.]
2\. Locate Great Slave Lake and follow the Mackenzie River from that lake to the Arctic Ocean.

➜ VIEW
1\. Look at the first two full spreads of photos between p.32-33.

2\. There is very early video footage of prospectors making their way to Skagway and over the Chilkoot Trail to the Klondike's goldfields. The following video shows footage filmed by Thomas Edison, who travelled there himself in 1896.
∞ Video Link: Thomas Edison's Stunning Footage of the Klondike Gold Rush

links.text: Thomas Edison's Stunning Footage of the Klondike Gold Rush

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
Saskatchewan, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Saskatchewan and Regina to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas, My Natural Wonders Map
There are no activities specific to Saskatchewan in Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, so we will add a few of our own.
1\. Turn to p.14 and look at the "Cross section along 50°N latitude." Did you know that the highest point between the Rocky Mountains and Labrador is in Saskatchewan? This is Cypress Hills, in the southwestern corner of the province. 
∞ Video Link: Cypress Hills 
2\. Find Cypress Hills on the map of Saskatchewan on p.36 and add it to My Natural Wonders Map.
3\. Did you know Saskatchewan is also home to one of Canada's largest sand dunes? In the second video, you'll see why Saskatchewan is called "Land of Living Skies"!
∞ Video Link: Great Sandhills 
∞ Video Link: A Beach That Doesn't Need An Ocean 
4\. The Great Sandhills aren't labelled on the map of Saskatchewan on p.36, but they are located between Leader and Swift Current, south of the South Saskatchewan River.
5\. Once you find it (hint: it's north of Cypress Hills!), add it to My Natural Wonders Map.

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Take out one of your blank maps of Canada and label as many places as you remember from the provinces and territories studied thus far. Then compare your work to the atlas or your notebook.

links.text: A Beach That Doesn't Need An Ocean

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
Wanderer
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from your reading last week?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1\. Vanished Beyond the Map
p.35-50 "After another winter passed" - "use of the original name."

2\. Read the Dictionary of Canadian Biography entry for David Hanbury.
∞ Article Link: Hanbury, David Theophilus - Dictionary of Canadian Biography

➜ VIEW
Review Darrell's route maps on p.39 and p.49. Add routes to My Arctic Map (optional); note that the map doesn't go far enough east to include the entire journey with Hanbury.

links.text: Hanbury, David Theophilus - Dictionary of Canadian Biography

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
Alberta
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Alberta
2\. What are the borders of Alberta? [A: Saskatchewan, Northwest Territories, British Columbia, United States of America]
3\. Turn to p.35. What is the capital? [A: Edmonton]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Columbia - 3747m - located on the border with British Columbia between Jasper and Banff]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Alberta in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Alberta using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a few bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
Travels with Hanbury
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map; My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week about Darrell's travels with Chief Yinto and with David Hanbury?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.50-62 "By the end of summer" - "such an improbable journey."

➜ VIEW
As you read, continue to follow the route by referencing the map on page 49.

➜ MAP WORK
Looking at the close-up map of Great Bear Lake (labelled here in German), find the major geographical points Darrell and Hanbury journeyed through in today's reading: Coppermine River, Dismal Lakes, Dease River, Great Bear River, Mackenzie River. You can add the names that are missing to My Arctic Map.
∞ Map Link: Great Bear Lake

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
Alberta, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Alberta and Edmonton to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.32-33 and p.35-36. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook.

➜ VIEW
Learn more about Peyto Lake.
∞ Article Link: History of Peyto Lake

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Return to Manitoba
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What stood out to you most from last week's reading?

➜ INTRO
Historical records—such as birth, marriage, and death certificates, government forms, property titles, etc.—can help to reconstruct the facts of an individual's life, but if you want to know what someone born 150 years ago was truly like—their thoughts, beliefs, and feelings—how might you find out?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.63-77 "By December 1902" - "he'd never return."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
Write a character sketch of Hubert Darrell. Include information such as: what are his interests, his values, his goals, his strengths, and his weaknesses.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 37
lessons.body:
British Columbia
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: British Columbia
2\. What are the borders of British Columbia? [A: Pacific Ocean, Yukon, Alberta, United States of America - Alaska and Washington]
3\. What mountain ranges go through the province? [A: Coast Mountains and Rocky Mountains]
4\. Turn to p.34. What is the capital? (Victoria) Where is that located? [A: Vancouver Island]
5\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Fairweather - 4663 m - located in the northwest corner]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of British Columbia in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of British Columbia using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, Vancouver Island, a few bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 38
lessons.body:
Travels with Harrison
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading about Darrell's time back in Manitoba?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.78-91 "Darrell's hard fist" - "for his greatest feats."

➜ VIEW
Study the map on p.85 as you read about Darrell's journey with Harrison. Add the route to My Arctic Map (optional).

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 39
lessons.body:
British Columbia, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add British Columbia, Victoria, and Vancouver Island to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.34. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook.

➜ VIEW
Emily Carr (1871-1945) was a Canadian artist who found inspiration in the beauty of Vancouver Island. Spend a bit of time today looking at the natural wonders of British Columbia through her art. Which is your favourite?
∞ Link: Emily Carr

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 40
lessons.body:
Delivering the Mail and the Northwest Passage
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading about Darrell's journey with Harrison?

➜ INTRO
In today's reading, you will learn about how Darrell encountered Roald Amundsen in 1906, when Amundsen and his crew were overwintering after sailing through the Northwest Passage for the very first time. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.92-104 "With nothing to keep him" - "he was off again."

➜ VIEW
Study the map on p.97 as you read about Darrell's journey as an arctic mail carrier. Add the route to My Arctic Map (optional).

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Roald Amundsen and the Northwest passage (1903-1906)

links.text: Roald Amundsen and the Northwest passage (1903-1906)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 41
lessons.body:
Northwest Territories
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Northwest Territories
2\. What are the borders of the Northwest Territories? [A: Yukon, Beaufort Sea, Nunavut, British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [A: Yellowknife]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mount Sir James MacBrien - 2762 m - located in the western part of the territory]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of the Northwest Territories in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of the Northwest Territories using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a few bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry in Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 42
lessons.body:
Dreams & Disappointments
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading about Darrell's journeys as a mail carrier and his encounter with Amundsen?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.105-117 "The hardened whaling captain" - "then all return together."

➜ VIEW
1\. Spend a bit of time looking at the remaining photographs between p.32-33. Note that several were taken by Hanbury and Harrison.

2\. View the Mackenzie River Delta from a satellite and try to imagine the challenges involved in navigating it during the spring thaw.
∞ Video Link: Exploring Earth From Space: Mackenzie River, Canada

links.text: Exploring Earth From Space: Mackenzie River, Canada

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 43
lessons.body:
Northwest Territories, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add the Northwest Territories and Yellowknife to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.37-39. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Bear Rock Sinkhole

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 44
lessons.body:
Guiding the Mounties
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from the reading last week?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.117-130 "Darrell himself would have" - "just beyond the horizon."

➜ VIEW
Study the map on p.124 as you read about Darrell's journey as guide and trail breaker for the North-West Mounted Police. Add the route to My Arctic Map (optional).
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about the Mountie's Lost Patrol of 1910-1911, and how the monuments to this tragedy are cared for today.
∞ Article Link: Lost But Not Forgotten: Remembering the Lost Patrol 114 Years Later

links.text: Lost But Not Forgotten: Remembering the Lost Patrol 114 Years Later

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 45
lessons.body:
Catch-up Day

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons or mapping activities in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings. You can also work on updating your Book of Centuries or History Chart.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 46
lessons.body:
Yearnings
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from the readings last week?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.131-141 "Darrell had been growing" - "Hubert Darrell."

• Reflect on how Darrell, whom history has scarcely remembered, was nevertheless known (both personally and by reputation) by so many famous adventurers and explorers during his own day.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION - Letter
Write a letter in the voice of Darrell to either his brother Charles or to Agnes Dudley. Or write a letter from one of them back to Darrell, asking him to come back to Manitoba instead of undertaking an "outlandish journey."

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 47
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Vanished Beyond the Map

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 48
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Canadian Student Atlas

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 49
lessons.body:
Yukon
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Yukon
2\. What are the borders of Yukon? [Northwest Territories, British Columbia, Beaufort Sea, Alaska]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [Whitehorse]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [Mt. Logan - 5959m - located in the southwest corner]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Yukon in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Yukon using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a few bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry into Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 50
lessons.body:
Darrell's Disappearance
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map; Canadian Student Atlas, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from the readings last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.142-151 "Reconstructing Darrell's movements" - "news for a time."

• Shoalts writes that at this point in the story, "I'd come to the end of his surviving letters and journals" (142). How do you think he will proceed in trying to solve this mystery? What avenues of inquiry are still open to him in this cold case that's over a century old? 
• What is your early guess about what might have happened to Darrell?

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.32-33, My Arctic Map
Referencing the drawn map on p.144 (Vanished Beyond the Map), follow Darrell's route in the atlas. Add this route to My Arctic Map (optional).

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 51
lessons.body:
Yukon, British Columbia, and the Rocky Mountains
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Yukon, Whitehorse, the Rocky Mountains, and Mt. Logan to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.40-43. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook.

NOTE: Haida Gwaii is the modern name for the Queen Charlotte Islands.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Watch a collection of stunning landscapes from the Rocky Mountains—including some sites you are now familiar with!
∞ Video Link: Best of the Canadian Rockies

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 52
lessons.body:
Traces
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week regarding news of Darrell's disappearance?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.151-162 "Back when I'd first started" - "to search for it."

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 53
lessons.body:
Nunavut
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, geography notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Nunavut
2\. What are the borders of Nunavut? [Northwest Territories, Manitoba, Hudson Bay, Davis Strait, Labrador Sea]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [Iqaluit]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [Barbeau Peak - 2616 m - located in the far northern part of the territory]

➜ MAP WORK
You will make a two-page spread of Nunavut in your notebook.
1\. On one side, you will draw a map of Nunavut using the atlas as a guide.
2\. Label the capital, a few bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. On the other side of your notebook, write at least 3 things about the province using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, vegetation, agriculture, industry, etc. 
4\. On the other side of your notebook, also write any additional information about the province, such as population, area, and date of entry in Confederation, using the information on p.10-11.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 54
lessons.body:
Faded Traces
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week regarding news of Darrell's disappearance?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.165-176 "Only once on any" - "summer of 2019."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Watch footage of a canoe expedition along the Anderson River to see what Darrell (and more recently Shoalts) would have seen. (content advisory: one scene with wine at a meal and one mild expletive)
∞ Video Link: Exploring the Anderson River

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 55
lessons.body:
Nunavut, cont.
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Nunavut, Iqaluit, and Mt. Logan to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.44, 45, 47, and 48. 

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook, including the chart of the island (Baffin Island is 1st in Canada and 5th in the world).

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 56
lessons.body:
Anderson River Expedition
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week regarding Shoalts's planning for his expedition?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.176-188 "When I arrived in Yellowknife" - "to his doom."

• Do you remember meeting Chuck Brill in the Alone Across the Arctic documentary? (If you want to revisit, his interview begins at 8:33)
∞ Video Link: Alone Across the Arctic

➜ VIEW
Spend some time looking at the first six photos in the second set of photos between pages 176-177.

➜ MAP WORK
Locate Norman Wells on the map below (it is not shown on the Canadian Student Atlas). Add it to My Arctic Map (optional).
∞ Map Link: Map of NWT

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 57
lessons.body:
Arctic
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Map of Canada, My Natural Wonders Map, geography notebook

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Add Baffin Island, Ellesmere Island, Victoria Island, and the David Strait to My Map of Canada.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Draw the Natural Wonders of Canada & Greenland, My Natural Wonders Map
Complete the activities on p.46, 49-52. You may wish to attach the photo to the area around the map with a number or arrow to indicate the location.

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add some facts about the Natural Wonders you mapped today to your geography notebook.

You've now completed My Natural Wonders Map! If you need more time or want to add more detail, the next class is a catch-up day.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
This week would be an excellent time to listen to Stan Rogers's folk song "Northwest Passage." Have you heard it before? How many explorers and arctic places mentioned in the song do you recognize?
∞ Music Link: Northwest Passage

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 58
lessons.body:
Catch-up Day

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons or mapping activities in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings. You can also work on updating your Book of Centuries or History Chart.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 59
lessons.body:
Anderson River Expedition, cont.
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from the last reading regarding the beginning of Shoalts's trip?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.188-202 "Many nights, sitting around" - "back to Norman Wells."

➜ VIEW
Spend some time looking at the remaining photos between p.176-177.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Sensitive content: includes accounts of a number of violent crimes in the Arctic from the past.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 60
lessons.body:
The Fog of Time
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What did you read previously about Shoalts's discoveries on his trip?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.203-212 "When I got home to Ontario" - "any trace of Darrell."

• What does today's reading show about some of the difficulties and frustrations of interpreting historical records, especially when they contradict each other?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about Bishop Isaac Springer, including photographs of him and his family. Unfortunately, the documentary described in this article was never completed.
∞ Article Link: The Bishop Who Ate His Boots

links.text: The Bishop Who Ate His Boots

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 61
lessons.body:
Canadian Geography Review, Scale Outdoor Map 1
⍞ Materials: Blank Map of Canada; paper or poster board (large enough for your outdoor space to be represented as 1 inch = 10 feet), paper, scissors, ruler, tape measure, pencil, and tape or glue

PREP: Choose an outdoor space to draw a scale map of. The space could be a portion of a playground, garden, etc. It needs to have objects in it (play structure, benches, pond, etc.) that can be measured and added to the map. Don't make the space so big that it cannot be measured fairly easily with a tape measure.

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
You have finished all the geography notebook assignments for all the provinces and territories, as well as completed My Natural Wonders Map! Well done! 
Spend 2-3 minutes or so reviewing your work. Then, put your maps away and take out one of your blank maps of Canada. From memory, label as many places as you can. This should include names of provinces/territories, capitals, bodies of water, mountain ranges, highest points, and anything else you can recall. Then compare your work to the atlas or your notebook.

➜ MAP WORK (scale map of outdoor space)
1\. Today, we will begin work on a scale map of an outdoor space. If you can't get all of today's task done, there will be two more classes to work on this.
2\. Using a tape measure, measure the length and width of your outdoor space. Write it down in your geography notebook.
3\. Next, measure the main features in the space and make a list of them with their measurements. (Things like benches, tables, pavilions, play equipment, etc.)
4\. Keep your list in the geography notebook, and next week, we will convert them to a smaller scale.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 62
lessons.body:
Travels to Smoke River
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What discoveries did Shoalts make from his archival research?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.213-225 "I stared at the map" - "what tomorrow might hold."

➜ MAP WORK
Locate and label Smoke River on My Arctic Map (it is drawn there but unlabelled). Use Shoalts's map on p.215 for reference.

➜ VIEW
Spend some time looking at the first four photos between p.224-225.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
On older maps (including the Canadian Student Atlas), the Husky Lakes sometimes still have the older name of Eskimo Lakes.

• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about the natural phenomenon of burning lignite deposits, which occur in several other places in the Northwest Territories near Smoke River.
∞ Article Link: The Smoking Hills
∞ Video Link: A spectacular phenomenon - the Smoking Hills Northwest Territories

links.text: A spectacular phenomenon - the Smoking Hills Northwest Territories

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 63
lessons.body:
Scale Outdoor Map 2
⍞ Materials: paper or poster board (large enough for your outdoor space to be represented as 1 inch = 10 feet), paper, scissors, ruler, tape measure, pencil, and tape or glue

➜ MAP WORK (scale map of outdoor space)
1\. Get your measurements from your geography notebook and determine if the scale of 1 inch = 10 feet will work for your map. (If it will not fit on your paper or poster board, you will need to adjust the scale.)
2\. Draw the outline of your determined space onto the paper/poster, making sure to note the scale on a map key.
3\. Using your dimensions from last week, make notes in your journal about what size they will be drawn using the scale you determined.
4\. Draw the features onto blank paper, which will be cut out next week. (This will make it easier to place them in the correct positions later.)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 64
lessons.body:
Husky Lakes
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map, My Arctic Map

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about Smoke River from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.226-238 "By far the biggest grizzly" - "narrow things down any further."
As you read, refer back to the map on p.215 to trace Shoalts's route. Add the route to My Arctic Map (optional).

➜ VIEW
Spend some time looking at the next six photographs between p.224-225 (from the grizzly photo to the seal photo).

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 65
lessons.body:
Scale Outdoor Map 3
⍞ Materials: paper or poster board (large enough for your outdoor space to be represented as 1 inch = 10 feet), paper, scissors, ruler, tape measure, pencil, and tape or glue

➜ MAP WORK (scale map of outdoor space)
1\. Get your drawings from last week and cut out the features.
2\. Carefully place the features in the correct spots on your map. This is the most difficult part, as you may need to go back to your outdoor spot and measure from the "sides" of your space and in between features, in order to get the most accurate map. (Note to teacher: The student will see the difficulty and hopefully appreciate even more how mapmaking is such a precise job. Don't let it stress them out; they will have to guesstimate to an extent.)
3\. Glue or tape down the features. You now have a scale map of an outdoor space. If you use tape, you could even come up with your own redesign of the space.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 66
lessons.body:
The Answer? 
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.238-250 "Finally, with calmer conditions" - "before flying home."

• What do you think of Shoalts's theory? Is it plausible? Could it ever be proven? What do you think is the role of intuition in the work of a historian?

➜ VIEW
Spend some time looking at the remaining photographs between p.224-225.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 67
lessons.body:
Legacy
⍞ Materials: Vanished Beyond the Map

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.251-264 "As we had made our boat journey" - "something well worth saving."

• Reflect on the contributions Darrell made to the history of geography. Shoalts writes that many times "in Darrell's life, the chance to go down in history slipped through his fingers" (257). However, his actions aided and affected many people, and he was on the periphery of a number of significant arctic discoveries at the turn of the twentieth century.
• What are your thoughts on the book as a whole?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
If you did not read the Dictionary of Canadian Biography entry last term, today would be a good day to do so.
∞ Article Link: Darrell, Hubert - Dictionary of Canadian Biography

links.text: Darrell, Hubert - Dictionary of Canadian Biography

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 68
lessons.body:
Local Map
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, blank maps, geography notebook, colored pencils, pastels, or watercolors

➜ MAP QUESTIONS (Local)
⍞ Map Resource: Provincial/territorial map (Google Maps)
1\. Locate your city or closest municipality. What part of the province/territory is it located in?
2\. Does your map show the counties? If so, locate your county. What are the bordering counties?
3\. How far away is your city from the northern border of your province/territory? Southern border? Eastern border? Western border?
4\. Locate and name the capital of your province/territory. Which direction would you travel to get there from your home? Why do you think your capital is located there? (Was it possibly located near a river for transportation and commerce prior to railroads, highways, and airports? Was it located in a more central location of the province/territory to make it equally accessible? Is it located at a major port? What other reasons could there be?)
5\. Which provinces or territories border your province/territory?
6\. Can you find a line of latitude and longitude that runs through your province/territory? (Not all maps will show these lines, and some will use their own grid system.)
7\. In your province/territory, find the bodies of water and watercourses. (Oceans, lakes, reservoirs, ponds, wetlands, rivers, streams, creeks, and so forth.) How many rivers/streams/creeks does the map show in your province/territory? Name some of them.
8\. Are any water features from your city or county missing from the provincial/territorial map? If so, why might that be? (Maybe there’s only enough space to show more prominent or popular features on a map of this scale and with so much content.)
9\. If your map shows physical features, can you tell what direction the rivers, streams, and creeks flow? If the map provides no hint of elevation or terrain, are you able to guess in which direction the water flows? Does all water flow in the same direction? Can you see smaller tributaries that run into larger ones?
10\. What is the largest body of water in your province/territory? How far away is it? Do you have any lakes in your province/territory? Locate and name a few. Have you ever visited one? If so, locate it and figure out how far away it is. How did you get there?
11\. Using the scale, estimate the distance of the closest water feature to your house. How far is it? Could you walk there? Drive there? How long would it take?

➜ MAP WORK
Follow the instructions in the right-hand column to draw an aerial view of your house. 
NOTE: There will be additional time next week if you can't finish this project in one class.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• MAP WORK
You are going to draw an aerial view of your house. You can use any medium you would like—colored pencils, pastels, or watercolors. (If you did this last year, try picking a different location, such as a favorite local site like a park or museum.)

1. Go to Google Earth and type in the address of your house or school. Decide how much you want to zoom in or how many of the other houses or buildings you want to include. This is more of an abstract bird's-eye map instead of a detailed map, since the view of Google Earth is not very clear or precise.

2. You may want to begin with a pencil drawing. Try to see the picture as a series of geometric and curvilinear spaces. A house, for example, is a series of rectangles as seen from the sky. Trees can be circles. Tell yourself you are painting patterns instead of detailed places. 

3. Add in the colors with your medium of choice. 
∞ Link: Google Earth
∞ Link: Example of a Pastel Drawing of a Ranch

NOTE: There will be additional time next week if you can't finish this project in one class.

links.text: Example of a Pastel Drawing of a Ranch

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 69
lessons.body:
Adam Shoalts on Vanished Beyond the Map
⍞ Materials: none

➜ INTRO
Adam Shoalts is an Explorer-in-Residence with the Royal Canadian Geographic Society (RCGS), and he recorded an episode of the RCGS's Explore podcast about his experience writing Vanished Beyond the Map.

➜ LISTEN, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Today, listen to the first 30 minutes of the podcast episode. If you are interested, you can listen to the remainder of the episode on your own time or during the next lesson, which is a catch-up day.
∞ Audio Link: Vanished Beyond the Map: Adam Shoalts on the mystery of lost explorer Hubert Darrell

links.text: Vanished Beyond the Map: Adam Shoalts on the mystery of lost explorer Hubert Darrell

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 70
lessons.body:
Catch-up Day

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons or mapping activities in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings. You can also work on updating your Book of Centuries or History Chart.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 71
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Vanished Beyond the Map

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 8 (Canada) - Lesson 72
lessons.body:
Exam Week

PREP: Print out a copy of the Blank Northwest Territories Map
∞ Map Link: Blank Northwest Territories Map

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Vanished Beyond the Map
Canadian Student Atlas

terms.content:
· Tell everything you know about Adam Shoalts's solo journey across the Arctic. OR Explain why Lake Manicouagan is so special.

· Fill in a blank map of Canada with the following provinces and their capital cities: Ontario, Quebec, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, Newfoundland & Labrador, and Nunavut.

· Explain why explorers like Darrell, Hanbury, and Amundsen could successfully survive in the Arctic climate while others before them (like the Franklin Expedition) failed. OR Imagine you are Hubert Darrell packing for a journey and can only take what you can pull on a sled. What do you pack?Reg

· Fill in a blank map of Canada with the following bodies of water: Atlantic Ocean, Pacific Ocean, Beaufort Sea, St. Lawrence River, Lake Superior, Lake Michigan, Lake Huron, Lake Erie, Lake Ontario, Hudson Bay, Baffin Bay, Labrador Sea.

· Describe the climate, vegetation, animal life, terrain, and any other physical features of northern Northwest Territories (between the Anderson River and the Husky Lakes) as it was described by Shoalts on his travels. OR Reflect on why, even with modern satellite technology, these regions of the Arctic are still so difficult to map.

· Label a blank map of the Northwest Territories with the following places: Beaufort Sea, Great Slave Lake, Great Bear Lake, Husky Lakes, Mackenzie River, Mackenzie River Basin. Bonus: draw in the approximate location of the Anderson River.
